reasoning: The handler at handleAjaxRequest() (lines 85-94) does use $_REQUEST at lines 91-92 instead of $_POST. However, the finding's claimed CSRF widening does not constitute a real exploitable vulnerability for these reasons: (1) check_ajax_referer() on line 87 itself reads the nonce from $_REQUEST (WordPress core behavior), so there is no inconsistency between the auth check and the data read - both operate on the same superglobal. A GET request with nonce in the URL satisfies check_ajax_referer just as well as a POST. (2) The CSRF-via-GET scenario requires the attacker to already know the valid nonce (wpdesk_tracker_deactivation_handler_{plugin_slug}), which is user/session-specific and time-limited. Without nonce knowledge, check_ajax_referer() will kill the request. (3) Even if fully exploited, the only effect is sending a fake deactivation telemetry entry (with attacker-chosen 'reason' string) to WPDesk servers. There is no data returned to the attacker, no stored XSS, no privileged state change on the WordPress site. The impact is nil to the site owner. (4) The capability check (activate_plugins) still requires the victim to be an authenticated admin. The $_REQUEST vs $_POST distinction does not bypass any authentication layer. This is a best-practice/code-style observation, not an exploitable security vulnerability with meaningful impact.
